projectTitle: REDD-PLUS results-based payments for results achieved by Brazil in the Amazon biome in 2014 and 2015
baselineDescription: GHG emissions from deforestation in the Amazon biome in 2014 and 2015 were used as the baseline for calculating REDD+ results.
mrvApproach: GHG emissions from deforestation were monitored and reported in Brazil’s REDD+ Technical Annexes submitted to the UNFCCC.
actionsImplemented: [{'action': 'Establishment of beneficiary registration and digital monitoring platform for Floresta+ M1 and M2', 'scale': None, 'status': 'in_progress'}, {'action': 'Direct payments to small rural landowners and land users for forest conservation and recovery', 'scale': '6 initial payments in pilot phase; project-wide, up to 797 (M1) and 1,239 (M2) eligible beneficiaries', 'status': 'in_progress'}, {'action': 'Finalization of ESIA and ESMP for the project', 'scale': None, 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Development of Floresta+ Operations Manual and M&E Plan with gender and safeguards measures', 'scale': None, 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Capacity building and participatory workshops with over 300 participants', 'scale': 'over 300 participants', 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Development and initial implementation of outreach and CAR acceleration strategies', 'scale': None, 'status': 'in_progress'}]
policyOrRegulatoryChanges: Implementation of the Floresta+ program operational instruments and monitoring provisions under national REDD+ framework.
institutionalArrangements: Project Advisory Committee (PAC) established with 11 government and civil society members. Project Board (PB) includes UNDP, MMA, and ABC as decision-making members.
primaryBeneficiaries: Small rural landowners and land users with validated CAR in the Amazon biome, including Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities (IPLCs).
projectSummary: This GCF-funded project supports Brazil’s achievement of REDD+ emission reduction results in the Amazon biome for 2014 and 2015 via results-based payments. It implements the Floresta+ program, providing direct incentives to over 2,000 eligible small landholders and IPLCs for forest conservation and restoration, with a focus on gender inclusion, monitoring, and capacity-building. The project delivers payments and technical support, strengthens environmental management, and develops digital tools for beneficiary tracking.
keyInterventions: ['Direct payments for forest conservation (Floresta+ M1)', 'Direct payments for recovery of Permanent Preservation Areas (Floresta+ M2)', 'Development of digital platform for beneficiary registration and monitoring', 'Stakeholder engagement through workshops and outreach', 'Capacity building and gender-sensitive interventions']
keyRisks: [{'type': 'capacity', 'risk': 'Limited number of eligible beneficiaries due to CAR data validation reduces project reach'}, {'type': 'governance', 'risk': 'COVID-19 restrictions caused operational delays and required activities to shift to virtual formats'}]
replicabilityConditions: ['Presence of an operational rural environmental cadaster (such as CAR)', 'Legal and technical framework for REDD+ results-based payments', 'Ability to reach smallholders and IPLCs in remote areas', 'Digital infrastructure/tool for beneficiary tracking and monitoring']
lessonsLearned: Accurate and accessible land tenure and environmental registration are crucial for inclusive beneficiary targeting. COVID-19 highlighted the need for flexible stakeholder engagement and adaptable project management. Gender data gaps require innovative estimation methods.

projectSummary: The Global Subnational Climate Fund (SnCF Global) – Equity, supported by the Green Climate Fund, is a blended finance facility targeting climate mitigation infrastructure investments across subnational regions, with Cote d'Ivoire as one participant. projectTitle: CRAFT - Catalytic Capital for First Private Investment Fund for Adaptation Technologies in Developing Countries
technologyType: private adaptation technology fund (equity and debt)
capacityOrScale: CRAFT Fund final size: $185.3M; Example company (Solinftec) covered 11.7M ha in 2022
financingNotes: The final fund size was substantially lower than the original target ($185M actual vs $400M planned). The GCF commitment is $45.32M at close; remainder is from private capital.

mrvApproach: GHG emissions avoided verified via validated third-party methodology (Control Union).

primaryBeneficiaries: Climate adaptation technology companies and their client organizations in emerging markets (e.g., farmers, agribusinesses using precision tools).
projectSummary: CRAFT is a blended capital fund investing in private adaptation technology companies in developing countries, with Brazil as a primary focus. The fund raised $185.3 million at final closing, including $45.3 million from the GCF and the remainder from private investors. Investments, such as in Solinftec (a Brazilian ag-tech), have led to measurable GHG emission reductions, fuel savings, and job creation.
keyInterventions: ['Blended equity and debt investment in private adaptation companies', 'Precision agriculture IoT/SaaS technology deployment (e.g., Solinftec)', 'Gender integration and tracking in portfolio companies', 'Monitoring, evaluation, and grievance redress mechanisms']

lessonsLearned: Actual fund size has a major impact on achievable climate adaptation outcomes; investor appetite shapes both the scale and diversity of interventions.

projectTitle: Ceara Urban Development & Water Resource
capacityOrScale: increase storage by about 8%; brings water supply to 50 unserved localities; about 30-35 earthen dams; reaches 150 microareas in secondary cities/towns; basin of about 7,900 km²
financingNotes: Weighted-average EIRR for quantifiable sub-projects: 16.4%. Loan finances all foreign and much of local expenditures. Institutional component mostly grants, with 10% municipal co-finance for technical assistance. Sub-loans with variable interest and inflation adjustment.
actionsImplemented: [{'action': 'Construction of 30–35 earthen dams and water conveyance works', 'scale': '30–35 dams, about 10 conveyance systems; relocation of ~2,000 families', 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Urban infrastructure upgrades in poor neighborhoods (Habitar program)', 'scale': 'Basic services in ~150 microareas targeting low-income population', 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Install ~120,000 water meters and new water/sewerage connections', 'scale': '~120,000 meters installed outside Fortaleza Metropolitan Region', 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Vocational training pilot and technical assistance for low-income youth', 'scale': 'Pilot program, US$2M for ABC vocational program', 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Institutional strengthening and capacity building for local governments and state agencies', 'scale': 'Training, technical assistance for 75% of municipalities and agencies', 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Establishment of decentralized water management entities, introduction of water rights trading', 'scale': 'Pilot in Araras Norte', 'status': 'completed'}]

institutionalArrangements: State Secretariat for Urban Development and Environment (SDU) lead agency; implementation by SDU and State Rural Water Resource Secretariat (SRH); State Bank of Ceara (BEC) as fund manager for project accounts; CAGECE and COGERH responsible for technical actions.
primaryBeneficiaries: Low-income families in secondary cities and rural areas (specifically, microareas with average income ≤ two minimum salaries); local governments and their water/sewage utilities; State agencies
projectSummary: The Ceara Urban Development & Water Resource project in Ceará State, Brazil aimed to improve water security, expand urban services, and strengthen local capacity. It financed the construction of 30–35 dams, expanded water supply to 50 previously unserved locations, improved basic urban infrastructure in about 150 poor microareas, and provided institutional support to local and state agencies. Actions included water storage and conveyance infrastructure, technical assistance, water metering, and a vocational education pilot for low-income children.

replicabilityConditions: ['Semi-arid climate with pronounced dry/wet seasonality', 'Regions with large share of underserved poor urban and peri-urban communities', 'Existing or feasible sites for water storage infrastructure (river basins, suitable terrain)', 'Local/state agencies with minimum financial/institutional capacity', 'Ability to mobilize multi-source financing (government, loans, limited grants)', 'Ability to implement targeted social tariffs and bulk water pricing']
lessonsLearned: Design should emphasize use of low-cost, appropriate technologies in water and sanitation; strong municipal capacity is critical for sustainable local infrastructure; effective decentralization requires clear institutional roles and adequate local government support; involving community and municipal authorities enhances planning and implementation.

projectTitle: Ceara Integrated Water Resources Management Project
capacityOrScale: Increased water supply coverage for 2.7 million people in Fortaleza Metropolitan Region; built or improved numerous reservoirs with up to 3,332 ha inundated in Year 1 reservoirs
financingNotes: Though tariff adjustments aimed for O&M cost recovery, major investment recovery depended on subsidies; overall financial NPV was negative, but economic NPV/IRR were positive.
actionsImplemented: [{'action': 'Creation and operation of basin integration schemes for water transfer', 'scale': None, 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Construction and rehabilitation of key water supply infrastructure (reservoirs, transbasin links, canals) for the Fortaleza Metropolitan Region and rural areas', 'scale': '3,332 hectares inundated by Year 1 reservoirs', 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Pilot watershed restoration and management in four micro-watersheds', 'scale': '4 micro-watersheds', 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Implementation of integrated water resource management and rational management systems', 'scale': None, 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Institutional strengthening: public participation mechanisms, user associations, management/monitoring tools, water use rights administration', 'scale': None, 'status': 'completed'}]
policyOrRegulatoryChanges: Establishment and strengthening of the State Water Resources Plan and Law, permit and tariff policies, water use rights, and effluent permits; enhanced regulation for environmental and social integration.
institutionalArrangements: Project implemented by Ceara State Secretariat of Water Resources (SRH), with the UCPE (Special Programs Unit) for management; supported by COGERH, SOHIDRA, FUNCEME; user associations and basin committees organized with public participation.

projectSummary: The Ceara Integrated Water Resources Management Project upgraded and expanded water resource infrastructure across Ceara, Brazil, focusing on the Fortaleza Metropolitan Region and rural areas. It built and rehabilitated reservoirs and implemented basin integration for reliable water supply, integrated management, and drought resilience. The project enhanced potable water access for an additional 2.7 million people, supported sustainable economic development, and improved environmental and health outcomes for urban and rural beneficiaries.

projectTitle: Gas Sector Development Project - Bolivia-Brazil Gas Pipeline
technologyType: 32-inch gas transmission pipeline (with 24-16 inch southern leg), compressor stations, fuel conversion support
capacityOrScale: 3,150 km pipeline (557 km Bolivia, 2,593 km Brazil); capacity up to 30 million cubic meters/day (MMcmd); 1,350–3,850 MW gas-fueled power enabled
financingNotes: Bank loan: $130m; capital market bond: $180m; Petrobras purchased $383m capacity option; total costs in Brazil $1.6b. Financing plan involves senior debt, sovereign guarantee, private equity. Debt coverage ratio minimum 1.38:1 (2002), average 2.84:1.
baselineDescription: Without the pipeline, South/Southeast Brazil would continue using high-sulfur fuel oil, wood, charcoal, and other polluting fuels for industry and power generation.
actionsImplemented: [{'action': 'Constructed 557 km pipeline section in Bolivia from Rio Grande to Corumba (with IDC and working capital)', 'scale': '557 km', 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Constructed 2,593 km gas pipeline in Brazil (Corumba to Porto Alegre), Penapolis compression station, land expropriation', 'scale': '2,593 km', 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Enabled and supported conversion to natural gas for industry, power, and urban heating in South/Southeast Brazil', 'scale': None, 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Supported federal gas regulatory agency, model concession agreements, and fuel price deregulation policies', 'scale': None, 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Developed and implemented indigenous peoples development and natural resource management plans along Bolivian segment', 'scale': None, 'status': 'completed'}]
policyOrRegulatoryChanges: Established federal gas regulatory agency and new regulations, model concession agreements for gas transmission/distribution, deregulation of hydrocarbon fuel prices, constitutional amendments enabling private and foreign sector participation, dismantled Petrobras monopoly (Hydrocarbon Law 1997)
institutionalArrangements: Project management committee of private and public sector sponsors; TBG organizational structure established; regulatory agency established with Bank technical assistance; privatization of gas distribution companies in key states supported by Bank.
primaryBeneficiaries: Industrial, power generation, and urban populations in South/Southeast Brazil; Bolivian economy (via export revenues); indigenous communities along pipeline route
projectSummary: This project financed and constructed a 3,150 km cross-border natural gas pipeline from Bolivia (Rio Grande) to Porto Alegre, Brazil, with 557 km in Bolivia and 2,593 km in Brazil. It introduced natural gas to South/Southeast Brazil, substituting polluting fuels and enabling new gas-fired power generation, improved air quality, and strengthened regional energy security. The project supported sector regulatory reforms, indigenous peoples’ development plans, and leveraged significant private investment alongside World Bank and sovereign loans.
keyInterventions: ['Construction and commissioning of the Bolivia-Brazil gas pipeline (3,150 km total)', 'Installation of compression stations and new gas distribution systems', 'Fuel conversion for industries and power generation', 'Implementation of regulatory agency and deregulation of the gas sector', 'Model concession agreements and privatization of distribution companies', 'Indigenous peoples development and land titling programs', 'Natural resource management and conservation plans']
keyRisks: [{'type': 'construction', 'risk': 'Delays in pipeline completion; per-day penalties imposed'}, {'type': 'market', 'risk': 'Lower-than-expected gas demand; possible IERR drop from 21% to 17%; take-or-pay contracts mitigate'}, {'type': 'resource', 'risk': 'Bolivian gas reserves may be marginal (80–85% of supply requirement proven/deliverable over 20 years)'}, {'type': 'regulatory', 'risk': 'Incomplete regulatory reforms or delay in establishing agency'}, {'type': 'governance', 'risk': 'Cross-country management challenges; need for robust institutional arrangements'}]
replicabilityConditions: ['Sufficient natural gas reserves to support 20-year supply contracts', 'Pipeline corridors/right-of-way through diverse municipalities and sensitive environments', 'Political will for regulatory and ownership reform in energy sector', 'Major anchor consumers (industrial/power)', 'Ability to mobilize co-financing from public and private sector', 'Viable transmission and distribution networks to end-users']
lessonsLearned: Strong regulatory and contractual frameworks—including take-or-pay supply agreements and minimum tariff guarantees—are essential for investor confidence in cross-border infrastructure. Complex stakeholder management and attention to social/environmental impacts, including indigenous rights, are critical for project acceptance and sustainability.

projectTitle: Santa Catarina Natural Resources Management and Rural Poverty Reduction Project
region: Santa Catarina (Center/Planalto and far West regions)
technologyType: integrated participatory microcatchment planning and rural extension
capacityOrScale: 880 microcatchments (~3.6 million ha), 105,000 beneficiary farm families, 850,000 ha under conservation planning or environmental protection

mrvApproach: Project-level monitoring and evaluation includes fortnightly measurement of 12 water quality parameters in representative microcatchments, and tracking of reforestation, pig waste systems, and implementation of microcatchment and watershed plans.

projectSummary: This project in Santa Catarina, Brazil, improved natural resources management and rural poverty reduction by implementing participatory planning in 880 microcatchments, benefiting approximately 105,000 farm families and 5,000 Indigenous Peoples. Activities included extensive training, rural extension, installation of thousands of pig waste treatment systems, reforestation of riparian forests, and environmental education. The project also supported new policy development, strengthened local institutions, and piloted watershed management plans for sustainable development and environmental restoration.

synthesisNotes: No direct tCO2e emission reduction estimates or verified results provided in project documentation. The primary sector is 'land_use_forestry' with 'sustainable_land_management' as the subsector, given focus on microcatchment management, reforestation, and rural poverty. Region described at sub-national scale (Center/Planalto, far West of Santa Catarina); no specific city information. Capacity is described as hectares managed/planned, microcatchments covered, and people trained; these are the most consistently reported scales across documentation. projectSummary: The PCF Sugar Bagasse Cogeneration Project supported two pioneering CDM bagasse-based cogeneration subprojects at Guarani and Alta Mogiana mills in São Paulo state, Brazil. The project aimed to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by replacing fossil thermal generation, targeting up to 2 million tCO2e reductions over 21 years.

projectSummary: This project in São Paulo state, Brazil, reforested about 2,500 hectares of degraded grasslands and riparian zones around ten AES-Tietê hydropower reservoirs using 80–126 native tree species. Financed through the BioCarbon Fund via results-based carbon payments, the project aimed to generate 400,000 Emission Reductions while delivering biodiversity corridors and water quality improvements. All areas were legally protected as permanent preservation areas, and reforestation methods and carbon monitoring protocols were developed and piloted.

projectSummary: The BR-GEF Sustainable Transport and Air Quality Project targeted the metropolitan areas of Belo Horizonte, Curitiba, and São Paulo in Brazil. The project delivered new urban mobility plans, land use regulations, information systems, and improved institutional capacity for cleaner, more efficient transport.

projectTitle: Financial Instruments for Brazil Energy Efficient Cities - FinBRAZEEC
technologyType: LED street lighting, industrial energy efficiency (IEE)
capacityOrScale: More than 18.5 million light-points countrywide; over 150 identified industrial EE projects
coFinanciers: ['Green Climate Fund (GCF)', 'Clean Technology Fund (CTF)', 'Caixa Econômica Federal (CEF)', 'Private sector lenders']
financingNotes: Financial instruments include syndication, partial risk guarantees, concessional and contingent loans. Primary funder commitment per catalog.
baselineDescription: Baseline assumes slow partial public rollout: only 30% of street lighting replaced with LEDs after 13 years; annual electricity for street lighting remains at 415 GWh, limited by municipal budget and COSIP surpluses.
mrvApproach: Subprojects measured by annual energy savings in Mega Joules and avoided tCO2e; cumulative value of debt mobilized is also tracked. CEF responsible for M&E and reporting subproject and guarantee fund data.
actionsImplemented: [{'action': 'Established Energy Efficiency Facility (EE Facility), including loan syndication for street lighting (ESL) and industrial energy efficiency (IEE) projects', 'scale': '$766 million debt for subprojects, $1,096 million total investment for Subcomponent 1.1 (program-wide)', 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Capitalized Guarantee Facility (GF) for credit enhancement (first-loss/partial guarantees up to 50%)', 'scale': '$25 million in grant capitalization (GCF + CTF), $200 million IBRD contingent loan credit line', 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Technical Assistance for CEF capacity and project pipeline preparation', 'scale': '$5 million (including $4M GCF grant, $1M GIF/ESMAP)', 'status': 'completed'}]
institutionalArrangements: CEF acts as project executor, lending, managing syndication, overseeing M&E, and operating the Guarantee Facility. Commercial/public lenders regulated by Brazil's Central Bank are eligible syndicate PFIs.
primaryBeneficiaries: Municipal governments (for street lighting projects), industrial/commercial entities (for IEE), and poor/vulnerable communities benefiting from improved services and lower energy costs
coBenefits: ['air_quality', 'public_health', 'jobs', 'improved municipal services', 'industrial competitiveness', 'increased financial sector capacity']
projectSummary: The FinBRAZEEC project catalyzed private and public investment in urban energy efficiency in Brazil through innovative blended finance. It established loan syndication and credit enhancement instruments managed by Caixa Econômica Federal (CEF) to support efficient street lighting (ESL) and industrial energy efficiency (IEE) projects, together aiming for about 12.5 million tCO2e lifetime GHG reductions. Technical assistance strengthened CEF's operational capacity and fostered a robust project pipeline.
keyInterventions: ['Syndicated loans via Energy Efficiency Facility for ESL and IEE subprojects', 'Capitalization and operation of a partial guarantee/credit enhancement facility', 'Technical assistance to strengthen CEF and prepare eligible projects']
keyRisks: [{'type': 'construction', 'risk': 'Low, as the EE sector was chosen for minimal construction and safeguard risks (e.g., bulb replacement, equipment retrofits)'}, {'type': 'market', 'risk': 'Low maturation and pipeline of bankable EE projects could limit uptake'}, {'type': 'governance', 'risk': "Reliance on CEF's capacity and coordination among multiple financiers"}]
replicabilityConditions: ['Mature commercial banking sector', 'Existence of public financial institution capable of syndication and guarantees', 'Pipeline of eligible EE subprojects (e.g., inventory of street lights or industrial retrofits)', 'Supportive regulatory framework for project finance and credit enhancement', 'Access to concessional and grant funding for initial de-risking']
lessonsLearned: Rapid project implementation depends on robust investment pipelines and TA at preparation. Competitive guarantee pricing and institutional capacity are key for replicability and sustainability.

siteContext.resourceConditions: Significant potential for energy efficiency in street lighting and industry

projectTitle: Mato Grosso Fiscal Adjustment and Environmental Sustainability DPL
baselineDescription: Deforestation in the Amazon region of Mato Grosso reached 1,749 km2 in 2018, a substantial increase from previous lows, with unchecked fiscal and institutional fragilities enabling expansion into forested areas.
actionsImplemented: [{'action': "Passage of State Fiscal Responsibility Law and administrative reforms ('Pact for Mato Grosso')", 'scale': None, 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Adoption of constitutional amendment EC 81/2017 to cap current expenditure growth at inflation for five years', 'scale': '5 years', 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Implementation of technical assistance for fiscal adjustment decision-making', 'scale': None, 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Implementation and acceleration of rural environmental cadaster', 'scale': 'Seven-fold increase in validated rural land cadaster area expected', 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Support and institutionalization of PCI (Produce, Conserve, Include) strategy and Institute for forest conservation and climate finance mobilisation', 'scale': None, 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Implementation of new Deforestation and Forest Fire Prevention and Control Plan (PPCDIF)', 'scale': None, 'status': 'completed'}, {'action': 'Economic incentives for regularization of rural properties (PRA), linking agricultural credit to environmental compliance', 'scale': None, 'status': 'completed'}]
policyOrRegulatoryChanges: State Fiscal Responsibility Law, adoption of constitutional amendment EC 81/2017 limiting current spending, administrative and payroll reforms, adoption of new Deforestation and Forest Fire Prevention and Control Plan (PPCDIF), reforms to rural land registration and agricultural credit allocation, institutionalization of PCI Institute.
institutionalArrangements: Mato Grosso State Secretariat of Finance (SEFAZ) managed program and monitored implementation; SEMA (State Environment Secretariat) managed environmental policies; PCI Institute recognized as key climate/forest agency; interagency agreements among Public Prosecutor, SEMA, and Public Security Secretariat for transparency in land registries.
primaryBeneficiaries: Citizens of Mato Grosso, including agricultural producers, rural property owners, and the wider public benefiting from restored fiscal space and environmental protections.
projectSummary: This Development Policy Loan in Mato Grosso, Brazil, supported state-level fiscal adjustment, institutional reforms, and environmental policy advancements. The project enacted a new Fiscal Responsibility Law, capped government spending growth, improved rural land registration systems, and implemented measures for forest conservation, including a new plan to combat deforestation and forest fires. It aimed to restore fiscal sustainability and increase institutional capacity for sustainable agriculture and forest protection in one of Brazil’s key Amazon states.
keyInterventions: ['Fiscal Responsibility Law and administrative reforms', 'Expenditure containment and revenue enhancement', 'Acceleration of rural environmental cadaster implementation', 'Policy reforms linking agricultural credit to validated land registration', 'Implementation of Deforestation and Forest Fire Prevention and Control Plan (PPCDIF)', 'Economic incentives for regularization (PRA)', 'Institutionalization of PCI Institute']

projectSummary: This World Bank-funded project provides a credit line and technical assistance to municipalities in Paraná, Santa Catarina, and Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil, to support investments in resilient urban infrastructure and disaster risk reduction. projectSummary: The Integrated Sustainable Mobility Project in the Foz do Rio Itajaí Region aims to transform urban mobility across 11 municipalities in Santa Catarina, Brazil. It seeks to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, improve accessibility and inclusion for vulnerable groups, create jobs, and improve regional resilience.

projectSummary: The Pozo Almonte and Calama Solar Photovoltaic Power Project involved building and operating two industrial-scale solar PV facilities near Pozo Almonte (7.5 MW and 16 MW) and a 1.1 MW plant near Calama, in arid desert regions of northern Chile. The project supplies renewable electricity to major mining companies (Collahuasi and Codelco) under long-term power purchase agreements, replacing conventional energy sources and reducing CO2 emissions by approximately 56,000 tons annually. It also served as a demonstration for large-scale solar to industrial offtakers in Chile.

projectTitle: Espejo de Tarapacá

capacityOrScale: 300 MW pumped storage hydropower and 561 MW photovoltaic (PV), 1,500 GWh/year low-emission power supply

policyOrRegulatoryChanges: Active advocacy for creation of enabling regulatory framework to recognize power system and social benefits of bulk energy storage (including PPA auction modifications and grid flexibility compensation proposals); actual regulatory changes delayed.
institutionalArrangements: Project governed by board of directors (including independent director); internal GCF/MUFG-compliant policies; executed by Energia Valhalla SpA and Tarapaca Fund Limited Partnership with GCF anchor equity and MUFG as accredited entity.
primaryBeneficiaries: Vulnerable local fishing and kelp-gathering communities in Tarapacá Region; broader national grid users
projectSummary: The Espejo de Tarapacá project planned to install 300 MW seawater pumped storage hydropower and 561 MW solar PV in the Tarapacá Region of northern Chile. Through large-scale renewable energy and desalination, it aimed to deliver 1,500 GWh/year of low-emission, resilient power and stable water supplies to local communities, with 35 million tCO2e lifetime emission reductions, but the project was ultimately cancelled before full implementation.

projectSummary: The Hornitos project in Chile developed a 55 MW run-of-river hydropower plant on the Juncal River, upstream of the existing Chacabuquito plant. The project included major civil works (channels, tunnels, a small reservoir) and a 10 km transmission line upgrade to 220 kV. It aimed to reduce emissions by displacing thermal power, generating approximately 2.34 million tCO2e reductions over 10 years, with CERs sold to the NCDMF.

projectSummary: The Quilleco Hydropower Project in Bío-Bío, Chile, constructed a new 70 MW run-of-river hydropower plant on the Laja River east of Los Angeles, with an average generation of 422 GWh/year. projectTitle: Chile REDD+ Emissions Reductions Program
capacityOrScale: direct intervention of at least 264,800 hectares between 2017 and 2025

baselineDescription: Baseline is emissions in the period 2001–2013; target is to reduce GHG emissions from degradation and deforestation by 20% by 2025, plus increased forest carbon sink capacity.

policyOrRegulatoryChanges: Establishment and official approval of ENCCRV as Chile’s national strategy for climate change and vegetation resources management (Decree No. 50 signed October 31, 2017).
institutionalArrangements: Execution by Agencia Chilena de Cooperacion Internacional para el Desarrollo (AGCID) and Instituto Forestal (INFOR), with core implementation by Corporacion Nacional Forestal (CONAF) at both central and regional levels, oversight by Office of the Comptroller General (CGR), governance by national (CTICC) and regional (CORECC) climate committees.

projectSummary: The Chile REDD+ Emissions Reductions Program aims to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from deforestation and forest degradation and enhance the carbon sink capacity of forests. projectTitle: Chile Green Hydrogen Facility to Support a Green, Resilient and Inclusive Economic Development
capacityOrScale: estimated 210 MW electrolyzer capacity
financingNotes: Private finance is estimated to be leveraged (approx. US$250m), but not yet confirmed nor attributed to co-financiers. Facility finances up to 25% of investment costs for subprojects. Blended finance is employed to reduce risk and unlock private capital.

projectTitle: Chile's Water Transition
financingNotes: World Bank financing (US$250M) is a portion of total program costs (US$1.546B). The program is government-led with World Bank support via disbursement-linked indicators. No explicit co-financiers noted.
baselineDescription: Baseline scenario: without the program, Chile would continue to experience institutional and infrastructure gaps in water governance, limited access to safely managed rural water services, inadequate climate resilience, and higher vulnerability to droughts and floods.
mrvApproach: Results are measured through Disbursement Linked Indicators (DLIs); verification by an Independent Verification Agent (IVA; planned as a university), consolidated program monitoring reports, and technical/quantitative indicators (e.g., people with water service access, flood protection).
actionsImplemented: [{'action': 'Creation and strengthening of river basin governance structures and working groups', 'scale': None, 'status': 'in_progress'}, {'action': 'Implementation of national and basin-level integrated water resources management (IWRM)', 'scale': None, 'status': 'in_progress'}, {'action': 'Expansion of access to safely managed rural drinking water and sanitation services', 'scale': '99,100 people to gain access to safely managed rural drinking water services', 'status': 'planned'}, {'action': 'Development and integration of nature-based solutions into water infrastructure for climate resilience', 'scale': None, 'status': 'in_progress'}, {'action': 'Rehabilitation/construction of flood protection works to lower risk for populations', 'scale': '1,100,000 people at lower risk of flood due to new and rehabilitated works', 'status': 'planned'}, {'action': 'Institutional capacity building for water information, monitoring, and public data systems', 'scale': '226 tools for improved knowledge on water quantity and quality developed', 'status': 'in_progress'}, {'action': 'Pilot projects for alternative rural wastewater treatment', 'scale': '3 pilot projects conducted', 'status': 'completed'}]
policyOrRegulatoryChanges: Development of a National Water Security Policy proposal; introduction of participatory water governance at basin level; possible creation/strengthening of a higher-level water authority.
institutionalArrangements: Program is implemented by Ministry of Public Works (MOP) via DGA, DOH, SSR, and Ministry of Environment (MMA). THJ Committee oversees the just water transition; an IVA will verify results; DIPRES (Ministry of Finance) handles disbursement; DGOP provides planning support.
primaryBeneficiaries: Rural populations, communities in flood-prone or drought-prone areas, participants in river basin governance, users of national water information systems.
projectSummary: Chile's Water Transition program is a national-scale initiative to strengthen water sector governance, expand access to safely managed rural water supply and sanitation, and improve climate resilience via green and grey infrastructure. Implemented by the Ministry of Environment and Ministry of Public Works, and supported by the World Bank through a US$250 million results-based operation, the program aims to benefit over a million people with reduced flood risk and upgraded water services, while bolstering river basin governance, institutional capacity, and integrating nature-based solutions.
keyInterventions: ['Strengthening institutional capacity and data systems for integrated water resource management', 'Creating river basin governance structures with stakeholder engagement', 'Expanding and improving rural water supply and sanitation services', 'Developing flood risk protection and climate-resilient infrastructure', 'Integrating nature-based solutions into water and flood management', 'Promoting gender equity in water decision-making']
keyRisks: [{'type': 'governance', 'risk': 'Potential challenges in coordination among multiple institutions (MOP, MMA, river basin working groups, THJ Committee)'}, {'type': 'social', 'risk': 'Stakeholder engagement and community participation critical for successful basin governance.'}, {'type': 'reversal', 'risk': 'Excludes investments with significant environmental and social risks or potential for transboundary water impacts.'}]
replicabilityConditions: ['Existence of river basin governance/legal frameworks', 'Government capacity to implement results-based funding instruments', 'Institutional readiness for multi-agency coordination (MOP, MMA, Finance)', 'Available rural water utilities or committees for service expansion', 'Regions vulnerable to climate-related water stress (droughts, floods)']
lessonsLearned: Participatory water governance, gender equity promotion, and robust institutional capacity are essential for a successful water transition program. Key innovations include integrating nature-based solutions and emphasizing stakeholder involvement in basin-level water management.

projectTitle: Chile: Program for Universal Primary Healthcare Coverage and Resilience

primaryBeneficiaries: Chilean population reliant on public primary health services; participating municipalities and their residents; specifically, new users from ISAPRES, rural and vulnerable populations targeted for inclusion
projectSummary: The Program for Universal Primary Healthcare Coverage and Resilience in Chile aims to strengthen primary healthcare services by scaling up coverage, upgrading infrastructure to climate-resilient and energy-efficient standards, and improving resource allocation mechanisms nation-wide. By program completion, an estimated 187 municipalities, including 127 new PHFs, will participate. The program is supported by a World Bank loan disbursed on achievement of specific health and institutional results and features additional reforms such as a virtual health management platform and updated preparedness for health and climate emergencies.
keyInterventions: ['Construction of new and upgraded climate- and energy-resilient primary healthcare facilities (PHFs)', 'Development of preparedness guide for public health emergencies and climate risks', 'Implementation of updated PHC resource allocation and payment mechanisms', 'Rollout of Virtual Health Management Platform', 'Institutional and capacity building for municipal and health services staff']
keyRisks: [{'type': 'construction', 'risk': 'Nuisances from dust, noise, vibration, and odors during civil works'}, {'type': 'resource', 'risk': 'Generation and inadequate management of hazardous/non-hazardous solid waste, and wastewater'}, {'type': 'social', 'risk': 'Exclusion of rural vulnerable populations not meeting size criteria for PHF construction'}, {'type': 'social', 'risk': 'Increased demand and dissatisfaction due to inclusion of new user groups'}, {'type': 'governance', 'risk': 'Potential weaknesses in internal controls within municipalities; limited PforR experience in MoH'}, {'type': 'capacity', 'risk': 'Learning curve for new procedures and requirements for additional support in MoH financial/administrative subdivisions'}]
replicabilityConditions: ['Presence of a national health service system or coverage', 'Local capacity for implementation and supervision in health entities and municipalities', 'Sufficient demand or minimum population thresholds for new PHF construction', 'Ability to coordinate and strengthen data collection/reporting systems', 'Political support for health financing reform and institutional strengthening']
evidenceAnchors: [{'field': 'totalBudgetUsd', 'claim': 'The cost of the UPHCP for the period from 2023 to 2029 is estimated at US$2.58 billion.', 'pageOrSection': 'Page 27', 'sourceName': 'Project Appraisal Document', 'evidenceKind': 'quantitative'}, {'field': 'primaryFunderAmountUsd', 'claim': 'The WB will support the UPHCP with a loan equivalent to US$200 million for the period from 2024 to 2027.', 'pageOrSection': 'Page 27', 'sourceName': 'Project Appraisal Document', 'evidenceKind': 'quantitative'}, {'field': 'financingStructure.loan_pct', 'claim': 'The financing of the central government for PHC at the municipality level represents almost 90 percent of the annual budget for PHC.', 'pageOrSection': 'Page 16', 'sourceName': 'Project Appraisal Document', 'evidenceKind': 'quantitative'}, {'field': 'capacityOrScale', 'claim': "By the end of 2027, it's expected that 187 municipalities will have joined the UPHCP. 127 new PHFs are targeted for infrastructure investments.", 'pageOrSection': 'Page 23, Page 49', 'sourceName': 'Project Appraisal Document', 'evidenceKind': 'quantitative'}]
synthesisNotes: No direct climate mitigation outcomes or tCO2e reductions are quantified in the evidence. Financing structure derived from the description of PHC funding composition (Page 16). Co-financiers not listed; only WB and national/municipal funding demonstrated. Capacity/scale value and unit refer to new PHFs, as municipality targets vary depending on intervention and year. Some DLI amounts relate to specific disbursement conditions, but only total figures are used in key budget fields.
siteContext.geography: national scope across Chile, covering both urban and rural municipalities
siteContext.resourceConditions: variable climate risk at local level, inclusion of climate change and epidemic health emergencies in planning
siteContext.infrastructureContext: leverages existing National Health Services System (SNSS), with new and upgraded primary health facilities (PHFs)
siteContext.environmentalConditions: responds to local climate vulnerabilities; areas at risk for climate-sensitive diseases and extreme weather events
siteContext.notes: PHFs constructed to improved energy efficiency, climate resilience, and low GHG emissions standards
financingStructure.notes: Approx. 90% of PHC annual budget at municipal level funded by central government, remainder from municipalities (Page 16, Project Appraisal Document)
